title: Run a Stage 6 Science Fair
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 6 Science to run a science fair that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage through real investigations.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the science from Stage 6 to run an accurate, safe and exciting science fair for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create a science-fair plan with a live investigation, labelled displays, a safety checklist, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your scientific decisions.

stages.title: 1. Foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Units 1, 2. Build a display that teaches each idea with a model or diagram, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Live investigation
stages.prompt: Pick one investigation from Units 3, 4 and run it live: state your hypothesis, follow the method, record results and explain your conclusion.
stages.evidence: A completed investigation with hypothesis, method, results and conclusion

stages.title: 3. Connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links ideas from Units 5 to everyday life, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links to daily life

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your science fair. Explain at least three scientific choices, check that your conclusions match your evidence and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded scientific explanation

evidenceChecklist: A safety checklist for every activity
A connections wall with three links
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Observations, measurements, models and conclusions are accurate and checked.
Hypotheses, fair testing, recording and conclusions follow the scientific method.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the science visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate science words and evidence.

questions.question: Which organ pumps blood around the body?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: heart The heart is the muscular pump; the lungs handle air, not pumping blood.
questions.unitTitle: Humans and Animals: Your Amazing Body Systems

questions.question: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart are called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: arteries Arteries carry blood Away from the heart; veins carry it back.
questions.unitTitle: Humans and Animals: Your Amazing Body Systems

questions.question: In a food chain, the arrow points:
options: from the eater to the food
from the food to the eater
questions.answer: from the food to the eater
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: from the food to the eater. The arrow shows the direction energy flows, into the animal that eats.

questions.question: Which of these is a producer?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: acacia grass. Grass is a producer because it makes its own food by photosynthesis; the others must eat.

questions.question: Which of these is a reversible change?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Melting ghee. Melting only changes the state; the ghee can be cooled back to solid, so it is reversible. The others all make new materials.

questions.question: Which is an irreversible change?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Baking canjeero. Cooking makes a new material that cannot turn back into batter. Freezing, dissolving, and melting can all be reversed.

questions.question: A force is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A force is a push or a pull. It is neither a colour, a metal, nor a kind of light.

questions.question: The unit used to measure force is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Force is measured in newtons (N). Kilograms measure mass; metres measure length.

questions.question: Which material is the best electrical conductor?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: copper — copper is a metal with many free electrons, an excellent conductor used for wires.

questions.question: What makes metals good conductors?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: free electrons — metals have loosely held electrons that drift and carry current.
